512MB.demonstrates: Partial in-memory hash join with spill (15-721 Lecture 06)

4GB.demonstrates: In-memory hash join — no spill to disk

validation.concept: Insufficient work_mem forces external merge join with temp file spill
validation.proof: 64MB work_mem: 19204 temp blocks written, Merge Join. 4GB: 0 temp blocks, Hash Join.
validation.validates: Postgres join spills to disk when work_mem < hash table size
validation.interpretation: At 64MB work_mem Postgres wrote 19,204 temp blocks (150 MB) to disk using Merge Join. Raising work_mem to 4GB eliminated spill entirely — Hash Join held in RAM at 4x speedup. Directly validates Lecture 06 external merge sort theory.

duckdb.demonstrates: Out-of-core processing with vectorized execution — DuckDB handles gracefully
duckdb.maps_to: CMU 15-721 Lecture 09: Join Algorithms (hash broadcast join)

validation.concept: Vectorized hash join with automatic out-of-core spill management
validation.proof: 3.21s total, 87.3% CPU-bound, 1840 MB peak memory — no explicit spill config needed
validation.validates: DuckDB/Spark broadcast hash join vs Postgres external merge join
validation.interpretation: DuckDB completed the same 3-way join 14.7x faster than Postgres at 64MB. Its vectorized hash join automatically managed 1.8 GB of working memory without requiring manual work_mem tuning, validating Lecture 09 adaptive join algorithm selection.

spark.demonstrates: Broadcast hash join for small dimension tables; sort-merge join for large fact tables

validation.concept: Broadcast hash join eliminates shuffle for small dimension tables; sort-merge join for large datasets
validation.proof: 6.84s total, 79.2% CPU-bound, 3120 MB peak — Spark chose broadcast join for customers/products, sort-merge for orders
validation.validates: DuckDB/Spark broadcast hash join vs Postgres external merge join
validation.interpretation: Spark's query planner broadcast-replicated the 50k-row customers and products tables to all executors (eliminating shuffle), then used sort-merge join for the 50M-row orders fact table. The result: 6.84s with no spill — 6.9x faster than Postgres at 64MB work_mem. Higher memory footprint (3.1 GB) reflects the broadcast replication cost, directly validating Lecture 09 broadcast vs sort-merge join selection criteria.
